result.markdown:
# Bitcoin Prices: A Real-World Example

This example applies mean change-point detection to hourly Bitcoin closing
prices. The source is the
[Bitcoin Historical Data](https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/mczielinski/bitcoin-historical-data)
dataset on Kaggle, which contains minute-level OHLC prices and trading volume.

Because the source file is large and is updated over time, the code in this
chapter is not run automatically when the book is rendered. Run the chunks
interactively to reproduce the analysis with the version of the dataset that
you download.

## Download the Data

Install the R dependency once:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
install.packages("data.table")
```
:::


Download `btcusd_1-min_data.csv` from the Kaggle dataset page and save it in a
`data` directory at the root of this project. Alternatively, users of the
[Kaggle command-line interface](https://github.com/Kaggle/kaggle-cli) can run:


::: {.cell}

```{.bash .cell-code}
kaggle datasets download \
  -d mczielinski/bitcoin-historical-data \
  -f btcusd_1-min_data.csv \
  -p data \
  --unzip
```
:::


## Read and Clean the Minute Data

The timestamps are Unix seconds in UTC. We retain observations from 1 January
2017 onward and remove minute records without complete OHLC prices. Missing
minutes can occur when an exchange or its API is unavailable.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
library(data.table)
library(scanr)

csv_file <- file.path("data", "btcusd_1-min_data.csv")

if (!file.exists(csv_file)) {
  stop("Download btcusd_1-min_data.csv to the project's data directory.")
}

required_columns <- c(
  "Timestamp", "Open", "High", "Low", "Close", "Volume"
)

btc_minute <- fread(csv_file, select = required_columns)

if (!all(required_columns %in% names(btc_minute))) {
  stop("The CSV does not contain the expected OHLC and volume columns.")
}

btc_minute[, Datetime := as.POSIXct(
  Timestamp,
  origin = "1970-01-01",
  tz = "UTC"
)]

start_date <- as.POSIXct("2017-01-01", tz = "UTC")
btc_minute <- btc_minute[
  Datetime >= start_date & complete.cases(Open, High, Low, Close)
]

btc_minute[1:5]
```
:::


## Aggregate to Hourly Prices

For each UTC hour, we use the first open, maximum high, minimum low, last
close, and total volume. This is the standard OHLC aggregation and reduces the
series to a more manageable size for change-point detection.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
btc_minute[, Hour := as.POSIXct(
  floor(as.numeric(Datetime) / 3600) * 3600,
  origin = "1970-01-01",
  tz = "UTC"
)]

setorder(btc_minute, Datetime)

btc_hourly <- btc_minute[, .(
  Open = first(Open),
  High = max(High),
  Low = min(Low),
  Close = last(Close),
  Volume = sum(Volume, na.rm = TRUE)
), by = .(Datetime = Hour)]

btc_hourly <- btc_hourly[complete.cases(btc_hourly)]

btc_hourly[1:5]
dim(btc_hourly)
```
:::


## Choose Window Sizes

Following the Python analysis, the largest candidate window is
\(\lfloor n^{2/3}\rfloor\). We sample 15 distinct window sizes between 100
hours and that upper bound. Setting the seed makes the R selection
reproducible.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
n <- nrow(btc_hourly)
upper <- floor(n^(2 / 3))
candidate_windows <- seq.int(100L, upper)

if (length(candidate_windows) < 15L) {
  stop("The hourly series is too short to sample 15 candidate windows.")
}

set.seed(100)
window_sizes <- sort(sample(candidate_windows, size = 15L, replace = FALSE))
window_sizes
```
:::


The closing prices are standardized before detection. Standardization changes
their units but preserves the locations of mean shifts.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
x <- as.numeric(scale(btc_hourly$Close))
```
:::


## Detect Changes in Mean

The Python voting threshold of 0.625 corresponds to `vote_threshold = 0.625`
in `scan_cpd()`. The R interface uses `n_boot` and `random_state` for bootstrap
calibration and reproducibility.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
fit_btc <- scan_cpd(
  x,
  window_sizes = window_sizes,
  n_boot = 1000,
  vote_threshold = 0.625,
  random_state = 100,
  change_type = "mean",
  n_jobs = 1
)

fit_btc
```
:::


The fitted object stores change points as positions in the hourly series. We
can map those positions back to UTC dates and observed prices.


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
detected_changes <- btc_hourly[
  fit_btc$change_points,
  .(Datetime, Close)
]

detected_changes
```
:::



::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
vis_change_points(
  x,
  fit_btc,
  index = btc_hourly$Datetime,
  x_label = "Date (UTC)",
  y_label = "Standardized hourly closing price",
  title = "Mean changes in hourly Bitcoin closing prices"
)
```
:::


::: {.callout-caution}
Bitcoin prices are strongly trending and serially dependent. Detected changes
identify shifts in the mean price level; they should not be interpreted as
causal market events. For a volatility-focused analysis, apply the method to
log returns and use `change_type = "var"` or `"distribution"`.
:::
